mnemonics.title: HAL Components
mnemonics.content: PARE - Peripheral Abstraction, Register Mapping, Endianness Handling, Standard Interfaces

mnemonics.title: Portable Code Checklist
mnemonics.content: VSTD - Use volatile, Use stdint.h types, Use sizeof, Define conditional compilation

acronyms.usage: Provides hardware-independent API for peripherals like GPIO, UART, SPI
Standard software interface for ARM Cortex-M microcontrollers
Memory-mapped registers that control peripherals in microcontrollers
Function executed in response to hardware interrupts
The lowest-order byte/bit in multi-byte data; opposite is MSE/MSB

visualTips.visualization: Think of writing a phone number '1234' on paper: little-endian writes '4321' from right-to-left (LSB at start), big-endian writes '1234' left-to-right (MSB at start)
Picture microcontroller memory as apartment building: Flash (program storage) is permanent storage like warehouse, RAM is temporary workspace like desks that clear on power-off
HAL is like a universal remote: buttons (API) are the same, but the electronics inside (hardware) differ for each TV (microcontroller)
Think of volatile as a 'do not optimize' sticky note on a variable that hardware can change at any time, like a doorbell - you can't predict when it will ring

analogies.analogy: Like writing a recipe using '1 cup' instead of '1 specific brand's measuring cup' - the recipe works in any kitchen (hardware)
Writing phone number as 'four-five-six-seven' (big-endian: most significant first) vs 'seven-six-five-four' (little-endian: least significant first)
HAL is like Google Maps - you input destination (function call), and it handles different roads/hardware (different microcontrollers) to get you there
volatile is like a live scoreboard at a stadium - the compiler can't cache/optimize it because it changes unexpectedly from external source (hardware)
